lesson.title: Mean, Median, Mode & Range
lesson.body: **Mean**: sum of all values ÷ count. **Median**: the middle value when sorted (average of two middle values for even count). **Mode**: the value(s) that appear most often (can be multiple or none). **Range**: max − min. Each measure tells a different story: mean is sensitive to outliers; median is resistant; mode shows frequency. Example for {2, 4, 4, 6, 8}: mean=4.8, median=4, mode=4, range=6.

rooms.question: Find the mean of: 12, 8, 15, 7, 3
rooms.explanation: Mean = 45 ÷ 5 = 9. The 8 error computes the median instead. The 15 error picks the maximum. Always sum all values first, then divide by the count of values.

rooms.question: Find the median of: 5, 1, 8, 3, 9, 2, 6
rooms.explanation: Sort first: 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9. With 7 values, the middle is the 4th: 5. The 3 answer picks the 3rd value (forgetting to sort first or miscounting). The mean is (1+2+3+5+6+8+9)/7 = 34/7 ≈ 4.86 — different from the median.

rooms.question: A data set: 4, 7, 2, 7, 9, 3, 7, 1. What is the mode?
rooms.explanation: 7 appears three times (more than any other value). Mode = 7. The 5.5 is the median of this sorted set: 1,2,3,4,7,7,7,9 → average of 4th and 5th values = (4+7)/2 = 5.5. A data set can have one mode, multiple modes, or no mode (if all values appear equally often).

rooms.question: A student scores 72, 85, 91, 68, 95. An outlier of 12 is added. How does this affect the mean vs median?
answers: Mean decreases significantly; median decreases slightly
rooms.explanation: Mean drops from 82.2 to 70.5 (large change — outlier pulls mean down). Median drops from 85 to 78.5 (smaller change). The mean is sensitive to outliers because it uses every value in the calculation; the median only uses the middle position.

rooms.question: Order these data sets by their range, smallest to largest:
rooms.explanation: Range = max − min: set 1: 10−3=7... wait, {3,5,5,7}: 7−3=4. Set 2: 10−1=9. Set 3: 10−8=2. Set 4: 14−2=12. Order: 2 < 4 < 9 < 12. A large range indicates more spread; a small range indicates values clustered together.

boss.name: The Median Lich
boss.taunt: Find the center — of your defeat.

questions.question: The mean of 5 numbers is 14. Four of the numbers are 10, 12, 18, 15. What is the fifth number?
questions.explanation: Sum needed: 5 × 14 = 70. Sum of four known: 10+12+18+15 = 55. Fifth: 70 − 55 = 15. The 55 distractor is the sum of the known values, not the missing one. The 10 distractor confuses 'missing value' with the smallest known value.

questions.question: A dataset has mean 20 and median 15. What does this tell you about the data's shape?
answers: The data is skewed right (tail on the right)
The data is skewed left (tail on the left)
questions.explanation: When mean > median, the distribution is skewed right — there are high outliers pulling the mean up above the center (median). When mean < median, it's skewed left. When they're equal, the distribution is roughly symmetric.

questions.question: Find the median of this even-count set: 3, 11, 7, 2, 9, 5
questions.explanation: Sort: 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11. Six values → median is the average of the 3rd and 4th: (5+7)/2 = 6. The 5 or 7 error picks one of the middle values instead of averaging them.
